role_hints.role: CAD drafters / BIM technicians (the production floor)
role_hints.oews_basis: 17-3011 Architectural and Civil Drafters 3.28% of the 4-digit 5413 wage bill — but 5413 is dominated by engineering firms; within 541340 proper, drafters/technicians are plausibly ~55-65% of wages (hypothesis; drafting IS the product here)
role_hints.note: 2D/3D production drafting is the product itself and the single most directly AI-automatable service in this industry set — text/model-to-CAD tooling targets the entire billable base, on top of pricing already deflated by offshore bureaus.

role_hints.oews_basis: 11-1021 General and Operations Managers 5.72% at 4-digit; owner comp largely SDE outside wages; hypothesis ~15-20% here
role_hints.note: Client account management with A/E firms and manufacturers; production oversight shrinks with automation.

role_hints.oews_basis: senior 17-3011/17-3013 occupations in the tail; hypothesis ~10%
role_hints.note: Drawing QA is increasingly automated (rule-based model checking); liability-driven human review persists only at the margin because the client's professional stamps the work.
Physical site presence for scan-to-BIM and as-built measurement — the defensible niche.

source_hints.source: Autodesk investor materials and product disclosures (generative CAD / AI roadmap)
source_hints.why: The dominant toolmaker's AI rollout defines how fast clients can insource what these firms sell.

source_hints.source: Deltek Clarity A&E Industry Study technology chapters (annual)
source_hints.why: A/E firms' outsourcing and automation behavior — this code's demand side.

source_hints.source: Published offshore drafting/BIM outsourcing rate cards (Indian and Philippine bureau pricing)
source_hints.why: The existing global price floor per sheet/hour that any AI-era pricing must be measured against.

source_hints.source: ADDA (American Design Drafting Association) member data
source_hints.why: The only drafting-specific body; may be defunct or minimally active — treat thinness of trade data as itself a finding.
Expect very sparse comps; absence of an M&A market for drafting bureaus is the data point for M and B.

capture_questions: Pricing is per-sheet or per-hour piece rates against clients (A/E firms, manufacturers) who already benchmark against offshore bureaus: when AI cuts drafting hours, is there any mechanism at all by which the bureau keeps the saving — or does per-unit competitive pricing pass it through immediately, as offshore substitution already demonstrated once?

Clients are A/E firms and manufacturers outsourcing overflow production — as those clients adopt generative CAD internally, does third-party drafting demand evaporate before anything else in this set, and is any client relationship durable beyond price-per-sheet?

Does scan-to-BIM / as-built field capture (requiring physical site presence) become the only defensible revenue line — how big is it within a typical firm's current revenue, and does it belong to this code or to surveying (541370)?
terminal_value_question: Drafting has no license, no stamp and no physical act protecting its core: the work is performed under the client's professional's seal. Mature text/model-to-CAD plausibly eliminates paid third-party production drafting within the 5-10 year window — the deciding mechanism is simply whether A/E clients' own AI tooling reaches production quality, since nothing legal or physical stops insourcing. Quantify what share of today's revenue the surviving niches (reality capture, legacy-drawing conversion) represent; do not confuse AI lowering the bureau's cost with AI deleting the bureau's product.
special_notes: Role-mix warning: the provided role_mix is 4-digit 5413 (engineering-dominated, drafters only 3.28%) and inverts this code's real structure, where drafters are the majority of the wage bill — rebuild and flag as hypothesis.

Pricing-model deflation candidate (double deflation): piece-rate pricing already deflated once by offshore bureaus; AI is a second, steeper deflation on the same revenue base — model pi_dist near full pass-through unless evidence shows otherwise.
No licensure moat of any kind — work is done under clients' professionals' stamps; there is no regulatory toll to capture anywhere in this code.

Acquirability reality check: n_band is 9 firms (the entire 100-499 class) out of 2,956; 2,510 firms are under 5 employees, and the record's 500+ class shows lower revenue/firm ($6.2M) than the 100-499 class ($21.6M) — an SUSB suppression/noise artifact. The honest finding may be that no meaningful acquirable pool exists.
Demand is doubly derivative: A/E and manufacturing clients are themselves automating, so the outsourced overflow layer disappears first in any downturn or productivity shift.

Boundary confusion: engineering firms with in-house drafting are 541330, surveying/mapping (where scan/as-built work may classify) is 541370, offshore bureaus invoicing from abroad never appear in US data — the code's visible revenue understates the true (mostly offshore) market for drafting labor.
